runs.t: Before writing a single line of code, good programmers plan their solution. This page covers algorithms, pseudocode, flowcharts, IPO tables and trace tables — the thinking tools of programming.

Imagine you want to make a cup of tea. You don't just randomly pour hot water and hope for the best — you follow steps: boil water, put teabag in cup, pour water, wait, add milk. That ordered list of steps is an
is a precise, step-by-step set of instructions used to solve a problem or complete a task. Every program you write starts as an algorithm in your head (or on paper) before it becomes code.
Think of an algorithm like a recipe. A recipe tells you
what ingredients to use, in what order, and what to do with them. A computer program is just a recipe written in a language the computer understands.

blocks.text: Key Characteristics of a Good Algorithm

rows.t: Every step is unambiguous — only one interpretation
"Do something with the number" — too vague
It must end after a certain number of steps
It must produce the right answer every time
Steps must follow a logical sequence
No unnecessary steps or wasted resources
Calculating the same total three times

runs.t: Before writing code, planning with an algorithm helps you:
Save time — plan once, code faster

In 1999, NASA's Mars Climate Orbiter crashed because one team used metric units and another used imperial in their calculations. A clear algorithm with defined units would have caught this. The mistake cost $327 million. Accurate algorithms matter.
— State it in your own words. What is given? What must be produced?
— Write pseudocode or draw a flowchart. Use an IPO table.
— Convert your plan into Delphi code.
— Run the program and check output against expected results using a trace table.
— Debug and correct any mistakes, then retest.
is the first planning tool you use. It forces you to identify three things before writing any code: what data goes
In exams you may be asked to complete an IPO table. Always write the Process column as a formula or decision — not just "calculate it". Show your working:

written in plain English that describes the logic of a program. It is not real Delphi code — it is for planning only. Anyone who understands programming should be able to read your pseudocode, even if they don't know Delphi.
Indent inside structures to show what is inside a block
Keep it short and clear — no Delphi syntax needed
Start with START and end with END

blocks.label: Pseudocode – Basic Statements
blocks.text:
SET total TO 10          // assignment
INPUT age                // get data from user
OUTPUT total             // show result
SET area TO length * width   // calculation

IF age >= 18 THEN
    OUTPUT "May vote"
ELSE
    OUTPUT "Too young to vote"
ENDIF
Example: Find the Average of Three Marks

blocks.label: Pseudocode
blocks.text:
START
    INPUT mark1
    INPUT mark2
    INPUT mark3
    SET total TO mark1 + mark2 + mark3
    SET average TO total / 3
    OUTPUT "Total: ", total
    OUTPUT "Average: ", average
END

blocks.label: Pseudocode
blocks.text:
START
    INPUT num
    IF num MOD 2 = 0 THEN
        OUTPUT num, " is Even"
    ELSE
        OUTPUT num, " is Odd"
    ENDIF
END

runs.t: A flowchart shows the steps of an algorithm as a
using standard symbols connected by arrows. It is easier to spot errors in a diagram than in written text, which is why flowcharts are so useful.

blocks.text: Worked Example: Is a Number Even or Odd?

runs.t: This flowchart takes a number as input, checks if it divides evenly by 2, and displays "Even" or "Odd" before ending.

shape always asks a Yes/No question. The two arrows leaving the diamond are labelled

an algorithm by hand. You pick a test value, then track every variable as you step through the algorithm line by line. This helps you check whether the algorithm produces the correct output.

runs.t: When two algorithms solve the same problem, we compare them. Three key criteria matter in CAPS exams:

rows.t: Steps must be in the correct order
Each step must have one clear meaning
SET total TO mark1 + mark2 + mark3
No unnecessary steps or repeated work
Calculate total once and reuse the variable

runs.t: The trick: assume the first value is both smallest and largest, then compare the rest one by one.

runs.t: A number is a factor if it divides another with no remainder (
). For example, 3 is a factor of 12 because 12 ÷ 3 = 4 with remainder 0.

if iNum2 MOD iNum1 = 0 then
  lblResult.Caption := IntToStr(iNum1) + ' is a factor of ' + IntToStr(iNum2)
else
  lblResult.Caption := IntToStr(iNum1) + ' is NOT a factor of ' + IntToStr(iNum2);

is a named storage location that holds a value during program execution. Think of it as a labelled box — you can put something in, use it, and swap it out.

runs.t: Decision structures let your program choose different paths based on conditions. Delphi uses
) is when a program tests whether something is true or false and then chooses what to do next based on the answer. A
is any question that can only be answered with
— for example, "is the age 18 or more?".
A robot (traffic light) at an intersection makes a decision for you.
the light is green you go;
you stop. Your program does the same thing — it looks at a condition and takes one path or another.

blocks.text: Why Do Programs Need to Make Decisions?

runs.t: Without decisions, a program could only ever do the exact same thing every single time it runs. Decisions make a program
to its data and its user.
you are old enough it lets you in, otherwise it blocks you.
a mark is below 0 or above 100, warn the user it is invalid.
a learner scored 50 or more, display "Passed", otherwise display "Failed".
the password matches, grant access; otherwise deny it.
Evaluates a condition (true/false) and executes code accordingly.
The IF statement is the most common decision in programming. In plain words it says: "
"If it is raining, take an umbrella, else wear a cap." The "if it is raining" part is the condition that is checked first.

blocks.label: Syntax
blocks.text:
if condition then
  statement
else
  statement;

blocks.label: Delphi
Delphi

runs.t: Combine multiple conditions in one statement.
let you ask more than one question at the same time.
to get into a club you might need to be 18
must be true. To qualify for a discount you might need to be a student
simply flips an answer around, like saying "if it is

rows.t: At least one condition must be true

blocks.label: Delphi — AND and OR examples
Delphi
blocks.text:
if iAge >= 18 then
begin
  if bHasLicense = True then
    bDrive := True
  else
    bDrive := False;
end
else
  bDrive := False;

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
if rgpGender.ItemIndex = 0 then
  sGender := 'Male'
else
  sGender := 'Female';

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
if chkInsurance.Checked = True then
  rCost := rCost + rInsuranceAmount;

runs.t: Tests if a value is in a set. Cleaner than multiple OR conditions.

blocks.label: Delphi

runs.t: Cleaner alternative to multiple IF…ELSE when testing one variable against many values. Works with
Use CASE when one variable could take many possible values and each value leads to a different outcome — like turning a mark into a grade symbol.

a vending machine. You press one button (the value), and depending on which button it was, a different snack drops out. Writing that as a long chain of IF…ELSE statements works, but CASE lays it out neatly so it is much easier to read.

blocks.label: Delphi

runs.t: Loops repeat a block of code multiple times. Delphi has three loop types — choose based on whether you know how many times to repeat.

is a programming structure that repeats a block of code over and over until a certain point is reached. Instead of writing the same instruction many times, you write it once and tell the computer how often, or until when, to repeat it.

Think of doing push-ups. The coach does not shout "do a push-up" fifty separate times. He says "do fifty push-ups." That single instruction, repeated until you reach fifty, is a loop. The action (one push-up) is the loop
, and "fifty" is the condition that tells you when to stop.
Loops save us from repeating ourselves and let programs handle any amount of work with the same short piece of code.
– printing the numbers 1 to 100 takes three lines with a loop instead of one hundred lines without one.
– you write the instruction once, so there is only one place where a typo could hide.
– the same loop can run 5 times or 5 000 times just by changing one number, so your program copes with any size of data.
– loops let you visit every item in a list, every learner in a class, or every character in a string, one at a time.

blocks.text: The Three Loop Types in Plain Words

runs.t: Choosing the right loop comes down to one question:
do you know in advance how many times to repeat?
climbing a staircase with a known number of steps — you count "step 1, step 2…" up to the top.
know how many times, and you must
filling a glass of water — you keep checking "is it full yet?"
each pour, and if the glass was already full you never pour at all.
know how many times, but you must do the action
asking someone for their PIN — you always ask once, then check if it was right, and ask again only if it was wrong.

rows.t: You know exactly how many times
Repeat WHILE a condition is true — check first
Repeat UNTIL a condition is true — always runs at least once

runs.t: Runs a set number of times. The counter increments or decrements automatically.

because the number of repeats is decided before the loop even starts and cannot change while it runs. You give it a start value and an end value, and Delphi handles the counting for you — you never have to add 1 to the counter yourself.
Reach for a FOR loop whenever you can finish the sentence "do this exactly ___ times" with a number — printing 1 to 10, working through all 30 learners in a class, or drawing 12 rows of a table.

blocks.label: Syntax — counting UP
Delphi — print 1 to 5
Delphi — countdown 5 to 1
Delphi — sum 1 to 10

runs.t: each iteration. If the condition is false from the start, the loop body never runs.
("pre" = before) because it tests the condition
, and only then decides whether to run the body. This makes it the safe choice when there is a chance the loop should not run at all.
Use WHILE when you do not know how many repeats you need
it might be zero — for example, "keep reading numbers while the user hasn't typed 0". If they type 0 immediately, you correctly do nothing.

blocks.label: Syntax

runs.t: the loop — otherwise it runs forever and crashes your program.

blocks.label: Delphi — while counter ≤ 5

blocks.text: WHILE — Reading user input until sentinel

blocks.label: Delphi — keep adding until user enters 0

runs.t: , then checks the condition. Guaranteed to run at least once. Loop continues while condition is
("post" = after) because it does the work
and asks the question afterwards. That is the opposite of the WHILE loop, and it is exactly what you want when the action must happen at least one time no matter what.

Use REPEAT…UNTIL when the body must run at least once — like showing a menu and asking the user to choose, or asking for a valid mark and re-asking until they get it right. You always ask once, then keep asking until the answer is acceptable.

blocks.label: Delphi — repeat until i = 10
Delphi — keep asking until valid mark

rows.t: Must run at least once (e.g. menu)
Process each element in an array

runs.t: Strings are sequences of characters. Delphi provides built-in functions and procedures to analyse, extract, and modify strings.
is the process of working with text inside a program — searching it, pulling pieces out of it, changing it and measuring it. A
is simply a piece of text: a name, an ID number, an email address or a whole sentence are all strings.

Think of a string like a row of bead letters on a piece of string, exactly like a beaded keyring that spells a name. Each bead is one character and it sits in a fixed position. String manipulation is what you do when you slide beads off the end, count how many there are, swap a small to a capital bead, or read which bead is sitting in position 3.
Almost every real program handles text. When you log in, the computer compares the string you typed against the stored one. When a website greets you by your first name only, it has
that name out of your full name. When a form checks that an email contains an "@", it is
a string. Learning these functions means you can validate input, format output neatly, and pull useful data out of messy text — skills you will use in every program from here on.
In Delphi, the very first character of a string sits at position
(not 0). This is why the reference string below numbers its letters starting at 1.
All examples on this page use:

rows.t: Position of a substring in a string
Number of characters in a string

runs.t: means asking "is this smaller piece of text in there, and if so, where?" The answer you get back is a position number. This is the first thing you do before you can extract or change part of a string, because you usually need to know
(Ctrl+F) in a document. You type a word, and the computer jumps to the spot where it appears and tells you where it is.
Returns the integer position of the

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
iX := Pos('e', sSourceText);    // = 5  (first 'e' in 'Andre')
iX := Pos('is', sSourceText);   // = 7
iX := Pos('xyz', sSourceText);  // = 0  (not found)

runs.t: Access a single character using square brackets and a position number.

blocks.text: Group 2 — Extracting Part of a String

runs.t: means taking a copy of a piece of the string without changing the original. You decide where to start and how many characters to grab. This is how you pull a first name out of a full name, a year out of a date, or an area code out of a phone number.
Think of cutting a slice out of a loaf of bread. The loaf (the original string) stays whole; you just take a slice from a chosen spot of a chosen thickness.
takes a single thin slice — just one character.
Extracts a section of text starting at a position for a given number of characters.

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
sNewText := Copy(sSourceText, 10, 4);  // = 'cute'  (start at 10, take 4)
sNewText := Copy(sSourceText, 1, 5);  // = 'Andre' (start at 1, take 5)

runs.t: a string means actually modifying it — adding text in, taking text out, or measuring it. Unlike extracting, these
procedures change the original string directly, so afterwards the string is different from before.
This is like editing a sentence in your exercise book.
squeezes a new word into the middle of the sentence;
is just counting how many letters the sentence now has.
Inserts text into an existing string at a given position. Modifies the string directly.

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
Insert('very ', sSourceText, 10);
// sSourceText is now: 'Andre is very cute'

runs.t: Removes a section of text from a string. Modifies the string directly.

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
Delete(sSourceText, 9, 5);
// sSourceText is now: 'Andre is' (removed ' cute')

blocks.label: Delphi
blocks.text:
iX := Length(sSourceText);   // = 13 (length of 'Andre is cute')
iX := Length('Hello');       // = 5

runs.t: means turning all the letters into capitals (
). The most important reason we do this is to compare text
are three different strings, so if you force them all to lowercase first, they all match.

Imagine a teacher marking a one-word answer. Whether a learner wrote "Cape Town", "CAPE TOWN" or "cape town", it is the same correct answer. Lowercasing both the answer and the memo before comparing is how you make sure capitals do not unfairly mark someone wrong.
string — they do not change the original unless you store the result back into a variable.

runs.t: Understanding what digital technology and ICT actually mean, how computers process information, and the basic model behind every computer system.
are electronic tools, systems and devices that use digital data (0s and 1s) to collect, process, store and communicate information.
Examples: computers, smartphones, networks, cloud computing, databases, AI systems, IoT devices.

rows.t: All technologies that capture, transmit and display data electronically — people, hardware, software, procedures, data.
of ICT — the development, maintenance and use of computer systems, software and networks for processing and communicating data.
Raw, unprocessed facts with no meaning on their own
Data that has been processed and given meaning
"These are odd numbers in ascending order"

runs.t: — performs millions of operations per second
— does not get tired or distracted
— large amounts of data can be stored
— can perform many different types of tasks
All computer operations follow this cycle:

rows.t: Raw data is collected and entered
Typing on keyboard, scanning a barcode
Calculating a total, sorting a list

blocks.text: Benefits and Negative Impacts of ICT

runs.t: is the growing gap between people with access to digital technology ("haves") and those without ("have-nots"). Factors include:
Hardware is every physical component of a computer — the parts you can actually touch. Software is the instructions that tell hardware what to do. Together they make a complete computing system.

cards.name: Keyboard
cards.desc: Most common input device for typing text and commands

cards.name: Mouse
cards.desc: Pointing device for navigating graphical interfaces

cards.name: Touchpad
cards.desc: Built-in pointer control surface on laptops

cards.name: Touchscreen
cards.desc: Detects finger or stylus touch directly on the display

blocks.text: Hardware vs Software — The Body Analogy

runs.t: are hardware — physical organs you can touch. The
your brain generates are software — instructions that control what your body does. A computer works the same way: the physical parts are hardware, and the programs running on them are software.
Every hardware component falls into one of five categories. Data flows through the system in a loop:
devices connect the computer to other devices.

rows.t: Allow you to add data to the computer
Keyboard, mouse, touchscreen, scanner, microphone, webcam
You type, click, scan a document, or record a video
You read text on screen, print a document, hear sound
Any program runs — it's always using the CPU
Temporary storage for data being processed
You open a program — it loads into RAM
Permanently save data for later use
HDD, SSD, USB flash drive, memory card, optical disc
You save a file — it goes to storage
Allow computers to connect to networks
You browse the internet or share a printer

runs.t: Used to type letters, numbers, symbols, and commands
Types: mechanical (tactile click), membrane (quiet, cheap), wireless, ergonomic
Special keys: F1–F12 (function keys), Ctrl, Alt, Shift (modifier keys), shortcuts like Ctrl+C (copy)
typing a document, entering a password, coding in Delphi
Types: optical (uses LED light), laser (more precise), trackball, wireless
Actions: left-click (select), double-click (open), right-click (context menu), drag-and-drop, scroll wheel
navigating a desktop, clicking buttons, drawing in a graphics program

rows.t: Detects finger or stylus pressure on screen
Smartphones, tablets, POS tills at Pick n Pay
Passes light over document, captures image
Google Meet, Teams calls, security cameras
Converts sound waves into digital data
Number of pixels (dots) on screen. More = sharper image
How many times the screen redraws per second
Liquid Crystal Display — uses fluorescent backlight
Improved LCD with LED backlight — brighter, thinner, more energy efficient
Each pixel emits its own light — perfect blacks, best contrast
Sprays microscopic ink droplets onto paper
Home or office colour printing, photos
Electrostatic drum attracts toner powder, fuses it with heat
Bulk refillable ink reservoir — no cartridges
Layers material (plastic, resin) from a digital design

cards.name: SSD
cards.desc: Solid State Drive — fast, silent, no moving parts

cards.name: USB Flash Drive
cards.desc: Portable flash memory in a pocket-sized stick

cards.desc: Small flash memory card used in cameras and phones

cards.name: Optical Disc — CD/DVD

rows.t: Magnetic spinning platters — a read/write head moves over them like a vinyl record
Cheap per GB, large capacities (1–20 TB), fragile (moving parts)
Flash memory chips — no moving parts, like a giant USB drive
Durable, silent, faster boot times, more expensive per GB
SSD connected directly to CPU via PCIe slot
Found in high-end laptops and desktops
Flash memory in a portable stick
Easy to carry, easy to lose; 8 GB–512 GB common
Laser reads/writes pits and lands on a spinning disc
CD (700 MB), DVD (4.7 GB), Blu-ray (25 GB) — mostly obsolete
Fragile — moving parts can break if dropped
Uses more power — reduces laptop battery life
Uses less power — better for laptops
Operating system drive, main laptop drive

cards.name: Desktop
cards.desc: Powerful, upgradeable, sits on a desk; separate tower and monitor

cards.name: Laptop
cards.desc: Portable all-in-one computer with built-in screen and battery

cards.desc: Pocket-sized computer with touchscreen, camera, and mobile apps

cards.name: Tablet
cards.desc: Larger touchscreen device between a phone and laptop

cards.name: Server
cards.desc: High-performance computer that provides services to other computers on a network

runs.t: = your desk workspace — only holds what you're actively working on right now. It's fast to access but clears when you switch off.
= the filing cabinet — stores everything long-term, even when the power is off. Slower to access than RAM.
The higher up the pyramid, the faster and more expensive the memory — but there's less of it.

— the "brain" of the computer. Fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and executes them. Measured by: clock speed (GHz — higher = faster), number of cores (more cores = more tasks at once), and cache size. Example: Intel Core i5, AMD Ryzen 5.

— specialist processor for parallel tasks like rendering graphics, gaming, and AI. Can be integrated (built into the CPU — uses less power) or dedicated (separate card with its own memory, like NVIDIA GeForce). Example: your phone camera uses a GPU to apply filters in real-time.

rows.t: A small photo, a 3-minute MP3 song
A large hard drive, server storage

runs.t: Software is the set of instructions that tells hardware what to do. Without software, a computer is just an expensive paperweight. Understanding the different types and how they are licensed is essential IT knowledge.

Software is a collection of programs and data that tells a computer how to perform tasks. You can't touch software — it only exists digitally. Hardware is the body; software is the brain telling the body what to do.
System software manages and controls the hardware. The most important type is the
The OS is the boss of the computer. When you turn on a computer, the OS loads first. Everything else runs through it.
Think of the OS as the
. The manager organises the kitchen (hardware), assigns tasks to staff (processes), keeps everything running smoothly, and makes sure customers (users) get what they need.
— provides the desktop, windows and menus you interact with
— controls how programs access the CPU, RAM and storage
— organises files and folders on storage devices
— manages user accounts and access permissions

rows.t: Installed on a single personal computer. Used for everyday tasks.
Manages a network of computers. Controls who accesses what.
Built into a device to control one specific task. Very small.
Smart TV OS, ATM software, fridge firmware
Designed for touchscreen devices. Efficient with battery.

runs.t: Utility programs are small tools that help maintain the computer. They come with the OS or can be downloaded.
is a small program that allows the OS to communicate with a hardware device. Without the correct driver, hardware won't work properly.
Example: When you plug in a new printer, Windows downloads or installs the printer driver so it knows how to send data to that specific printer model.
Application software lets you perform specific tasks — it uses the OS as its foundation.

rows.t: Play games, watch media, listen to music

runs.t: When you "buy" software, you're not actually buying the software — you're buying a
to use it. The developer keeps ownership. Software licences define what you are and aren't allowed to do with the software.

rows.t: Skype, Google Chrome, Adobe Acrobat Reader
Free for trial period, then pay

runs.t: The EULA is the legal agreement between you and the software developer. It's that long wall of text you click "I Agree" on when installing software.
It specifies: how many devices you can install on, what you're not allowed to do, and who owns the software.

rows.t: One user on one device only
A specific number of users or devices (e.g. 10 PCs)
Unlimited use within one organisation (e.g. a school)

runs.t: is the legal right that protects a creator's work. Software is automatically protected by copyright the moment it is created. You cannot copy, share or distribute software without the copyright holder's permission.
is the illegal copying or distribution of copyrighted software. It is a crime in South Africa and most countries.
: installing one licensed copy on multiple computers
: making and selling fake copies of licensed software
: downloading software illegally from the internet (torrents, crack sites)
: selling computers with pirated software pre-installed
These are alternative licensing systems that allow sharing and collaboration while still giving creators some protection.
: You can use the work if you credit the creator
: You can use it, but not for profit
: Any work you create from it must use the same licence
: You cannot modify the original work

Computers only understand two states: on (1) and off (0). Everything — text, images, sound, video — must first be converted to those 1s and 0s before a computer can process it. This page explains how that conversion works.
Deep inside your computer, everything is built from tiny transistors — microscopic electronic switches. Each switch can only be in one of two states:
because there are only two options.
A single 1 or 0 is called a
). On its own, one bit can only represent two things. But group 8 bits together into a
, and suddenly you can represent 256 different values (2
= 256) — enough for every letter, digit, and common symbol.

Think of a light switch: it is either ON or OFF — that's 1 bit. Now imagine a row of 8 light switches. Each combination of on/off gives a different pattern — 256 possible combinations. That's how a computer stores one character.
Eight bits grouped together form one
. The example below shows the byte
— the binary code for the letter

rows.t: Everyday counting — the system humans naturally use
Internal computer data — all data is stored this way
Colour codes (#FF5733), memory addresses, shorter binary notation

runs.t: In decimal, each column is worth 10× the column to its right (1, 10, 100, 1000…). In binary, each column is worth
. Every binary conversion uses these numbers. Notice each one doubles: 1×2=2, 2×2=4, 4×2=8, 8×2=16, and so on.
Multiply each bit by its place value, then add them all up. Only count the columns where the bit is
Divide the number by 2 repeatedly. Write down each remainder. Read the remainders from

blocks.label: Example: 25 in decimal → binary
blocks.text:
25 ÷ 2 = 12  remainder  1   ← LSB (written last, read first from bottom)
12 ÷ 2 =  6  remainder  0
 6 ÷ 2 =  3  remainder  0
 3 ÷ 2 =  1  remainder  1
 1 ÷ 2 =  0  remainder  1   ← MSB (written first, read last from bottom)

Read remainders bottom to top:  1  1  0  0  1
                                                = 11001₂

Verify:  16 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 25 ✓

blocks.label: Example: 13 in decimal → binary
blocks.text:
13 ÷ 2 = 6  remainder  1
 6 ÷ 2 = 3  remainder  0
 3 ÷ 2 = 1  remainder  1
 1 ÷ 2 = 0  remainder  1

Read bottom to top:  1  1  0  1  =  1101₂

Verify:  8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13 ✓

runs.t: Hexadecimal (hex) uses 16 symbols: the digits 0–9, then the letters A–F for values 10–15. Because one hex digit represents exactly 4 binary bits, hex is a much shorter way to write binary numbers. For example,
Each position in hex is worth 16× the position to its right (just like binary uses powers of 2, hex uses powers of 16).

blocks.label: Example: 2A (hex) → decimal
blocks.text:
2A₁₆  has two digits:  2  and  A

Position:   16¹    16⁰
Digit:        2      A (=10)
Value:     2×16  + 10×1
         =   32  +   10
         =       42₁₀

blocks.label: Example: 200 (decimal) → hex
blocks.text:
200 ÷ 16 = 12  remainder  8    →  8
 12 ÷ 16 =  0  remainder  12   →  C  (12 in hex = C)

Read remainders bottom to top:  C  8  →  C8₁₆

Verify:  12 × 16 + 8 = 192 + 8 = 200 ✓

runs.t: Hex is everywhere in computing. Colour codes in web design use hex:
= red (255 red, 0 green, 0 blue). Memory addresses in low-level programming are hex. Even Delphi lets you write hex literals with a dollar sign:
A computer stores text by mapping each character to a number. The most common mapping is
(American Standard Code for Information Interchange). Each character has a unique number — a code — which is then stored in binary.

rows.t: The digit zero (not the letter O)

runs.t: The difference between an uppercase letter and its lowercase version is always

rows.t: 7-bit (128 chars) or 8-bit (256). English letters, digits, symbols only.
Variable length (1–4 bytes). Backward compatible with ASCII. Most common on the web.
Universal standard — 140 000+ characters including all world languages.

blocks.label: Delphi — Ord and Chr with ASCII

rows.t: One page of plain text (~1 000 characters)
A small JPEG photo, a 3-min MP3 song
A 2-hour HD movie, a mobile game

runs.t: = 1 024, which is close to 1 000 but not equal. So 1 KB = 1 024 bytes (not 1 000). Hard drive manufacturers sometimes use 1 000 to make drives sound bigger — that's why a "500 GB" drive shows as ~465 GB in Windows.
A computer network connects two or more devices so they can communicate and share resources. Networks are what make the internet, school computer labs, and your home Wi-Fi possible.

blocks.text: Why Would You Connect Computers Together?

runs.t: Imagine your school has 30 computers but only 2 printers. Without a network, only the computers physically connected to a printer could print. With a network, every computer can send print jobs to either printer — that's sharing resources.
Networks make all of the following possible:
— share printers, scanners, internet connections, and storage across multiple computers
— send a document from one computer to another without a USB drive
— email, instant messaging, video calls (e.g. your school uses Microsoft Teams or Google Meet)
— store all student data on one server so it's accessible from any computer in the lab
— one router can give all devices in a building internet access
— teachers can access school systems from home via a VPN (Virtual Private Network)
Your school's computer lab is a

(Local Area Network). Every computer connects through a switch to a router, which connects to your ISP (Internet Service Provider) — maybe Telkom, MWEB, or Vodacom. That router gives every computer in the lab access to the internet.

rows.t: Bluetooth headphones, Apple Watch connected to phone, wireless keyboard
Home Wi-Fi router (Telkom/Vodacom fibre), smart TV, gaming console, laptop all connected
School computer lab, office building network, university campus network
The Internet; MTN/Vodacom cellular network connecting towns across SA

runs.t: describes the physical layout of how devices are connected in a network. The most common topology used in school labs and offices is the
: every device connects directly to a central switch or hub.

rows.t: Easy to add new devices — just connect to the switch
If the central switch fails, the whole network goes down
One faulty cable only affects that one device
Requires more cable than some other topologies
Easy to diagnose faults — test one connection at a time
Cost of the switch adds to setup expense
Most widely used in schools and offices
Performance depends on the quality of the switch

runs.t: Data from your computer travels through several devices before it reaches the internet. Here is how they connect:

rows.t: Connects a device to a network. Every computer has one, either built-in or as an add-on card. Assigns the device a unique MAC address.
Your laptop's built-in Ethernet port or Wi-Fi card
Connects multiple devices within a LAN. Smarter than a hub — sends data only to the specific device it's addressed to (using MAC addresses).
The network switch in your school's server room connecting all computers in the lab
Connects two or more networks together. Routes data between your LAN and the internet using IP addresses. Usually also acts as a firewall.
The Telkom/Vodacom router in your home or school that gives Wi-Fi access
Converts digital computer data into a signal suitable for your internet line (ADSL, fibre, LTE), and vice versa. "Modulator-Demodulator".
Telkom ADSL modem, or LTE modem (like a Huawei Wifi router)
Extends wireless network coverage. Connects wirelessly to clients and wired to the switch/router.
Extra Wi-Fi routers placed in different classrooms of a large school

runs.t: provides services (files, printers, internet access, authentication) to multiple
computers. The server is a dedicated, powerful computer that is always on.
All computers have equal status — any device can be both client and server. No single central machine is in charge. Common in small home networks.

rows.t: Weak — each device manages its own security
Centralised — easy to manage by one admin
Decentralised — hard to manage as network grows
Consistent — server is optimised for the job
Variable — depends on each computer's specs
Easy — all data is on the server
Difficult — data is spread across all machines
If server fails, whole network is affected
One computer failing rarely affects others
School lab with a Windows Server; bank branch
Two friends sharing files between laptops at home

runs.t: is the physical pathway over which data travels. Choosing the right medium depends on the required speed, distance, security, and budget.

rows.t: Electrical signals through 4 twisted copper pairs. RJ45 connectors.
Good — hard to intercept without physical access
School computer lab wiring; office desk connections
Light pulses through thin glass/plastic strands. Not affected by electrical interference.
Very high — almost impossible to tap
Openserve/Vumatel fibre to your home; undersea cables connecting SA to Europe
Radio signals transmitted through air. No cable needed.
Needs encryption (WPA3) — radio signals can be intercepted
Low — just a router; no cabling needed
Home routers; school library hotspots; coffee shops
Radio signals through cell towers. Cellular network.
MTN, Vodacom, Cell C towers connecting rural SA; mobile data on phones
Infrared light — requires line of sight, no obstacles.
TV remotes, old phone data transfers (largely obsolete)

runs.t: Many South African schools and homes are getting
connections through providers like Openserve (Telkom), Vumatel, and Frogfoot. Before fibre, most homes used
over copper phone lines. In rural areas where fibre hasn't reached,
from MTN, Vodacom, or Rain is the main option for internet access.
The Internet and the World Wide Web are related but different. Learn how they work and how to use search engines effectively.

rows.t: The global network of connected computers
A collection of websites accessible via the Internet
The shops and buildings on those roads
Unique address identifying a device on the Internet (e.g. 192.168.1.1)
Readable name linked to an IP address (e.g. google.com)
Full web address of a specific page (e.g. https://www.school.co.za/it)
HyperText Markup Language — coding language used to build web pages
Secure HTTP with encryption — look for the padlock icon
Internet Service Provider — company that gives you internet access

runs.t: interprets HTML and displays web pages. Examples: Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari.

rows.t: Fake official emails stealing login details
Never click suspicious links; verify email address
Check URL carefully before entering passwords
Use spam filter; don't open unknown attachments
Malware that encrypts your data and demands payment
Regular backups; don't open unknown attachments
False information spread as if true
Verify with reputable sources before sharing

runs.t: The internet provides far more than just web browsing. This page covers plug-in applications, common internet services, and the technologies that power them.
is additional software installed into an existing application (e.g. a browser) to extend its functionality.

rows.t: Allows browsers to open and display PDF files
Allows browsers to display interactive animations and videos (mostly obsolete now)
Allows Java programs to run within a web browser
Stream and play audio/video directly from the browser
Enhanced interactivity for web pages (replaced by HTML5)

runs.t: Internet services technologies cover web development, web design, networking and e-commerce. They are used together to create functional, interactive websites.

rows.t: Structures and displays web page content
Controls the visual styling of web pages (colours, fonts, layout)
Adds interactivity to web pages (animations, form validation)
Server-side scripting — processes forms and connects to databases
Queries and manages database data for websites
Transfers files between computers over a network
Collection of web pages accessed via browser using HTTP/HTTPS
Electronic mail — send and receive messages
File Transfer Protocol — upload and download large files
Voice over IP — phone/video calls over the internet
Audio and video delivered in real time without downloading first
Store and access files on remote servers (Google Drive, OneDrive)
Manage bank accounts and transfer money via the internet
Buy and sell goods and services online

runs.t: sets the standards that all web browsers and websites should follow to ensure web pages display correctly across all devices and browsers. Web designers follow these standards.
Electronic communication covers all methods of exchanging information digitally — from email and instant messaging to video calls and social media.
Use a clear, descriptive subject line
Begin with a greeting; end with a signature
AVOID ALL CAPS — it looks like shouting
Proofread for spelling and grammar before sending
Do not forward spam or chain emails
Never send sensitive info (passwords, banking details) via email

rows.t: Most digital communication is free (data costs may apply)
Written messages reduce misunderstandings vs. verbal

runs.t: Sit upright; monitor at eye level
Keyboard and mouse at comfortable arm height
Use the 20-20-20 rule: every 20 min, look 20m away for 20 seconds
— be careful what you share online; it leaves a digital footprint
— turn off devices, reduce printing, recycle e-waste
Keeping a computer running well and securely requires regular maintenance tasks and an understanding of common threats.

rows.t: Removes temporary files to free up storage space
Reorganises fragmented files on HDD for faster access (not needed for SSD)
Automates tasks like backups and updates at set times
Reduces file size using mathematical algorithms (.zip)
Moves inactive data to separate storage (long-term, not duplicated)
Creates copies of data on a different device for disaster recovery
Complete copy of all files. Slowest but easiest to restore.
Only backs up files changed since last backup (any type). Fast, but restoring needs all increments.
Backs up files changed since last full backup. Compromise between full and incremental.

runs.t: — external hard drive kept at same location
— physical device kept at a different location
— e.g. Google Drive, OneDrive — automatic, accessible anywhere

rows.t: Replicates and spreads; performs harmful actions
Spreads across networks without user action
Disguised as useful software; gives attackers back-door access
Encrypts your data; demands payment for the key
Monitors network traffic; blocks unauthorised connections
8+ chars, uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters
Users only access files they are authorised for
Uninterruptible Power Supply — keeps computer running during power failure
Scrambles data so only authorised parties can read it

runs.t: is a collection of data stored as a single unit (a document, image, song, program). A
(directory) is a container used to group related files. Organising files means storing them in a logical, meaningful way so they are easy to find later.
describes exactly where a file lives on a storage device — the drive, the folders, the file name and its extension.

rows.t: The storage device the file is on
The chain of folders leading to the file
The name you gave the file
Tells the computer what type of file it is

runs.t: (the letters after the dot) tells the operating system which program should open the file.

blocks.text: Why Use a Good File Structure?

runs.t: — you don't save the same file in many places
Makes backing up specific folders simpler
(e.g. Windows File Explorer) is the software used to view, move, copy, rename and delete files and folders.

rows.t: Save the file under a new name, location, or a different version/format of the
output file in a different format for use elsewhere

runs.t: Organise files into logical folder structures
Avoid special characters in file names:
Use underscores or dashes instead of spaces
Back up regularly — at least weekly
Based on the 2026 IT CAPS Annual Teaching Plan. Practical (Paper 1) and Theory (Paper 2) topics per term.

blocks.title: Term 1 • January – March
blocks.subtitle: Foundations: computing, data representation, algorithms & intro to Delphi

cols.title: 🔬 Practical (Paper 1)
items: Algorithms & problem solving (IPO tables, flowcharts, pseudocode)

cols.title: 📘 Theory (Paper 2)
items: Bits, bytes, KB, MB, GB, TB
Social implications: licensing, piracy, copyright, digital divide, POPIA

cols.title: 🔬 Practical (Paper 1)
items: Boolean operators: AND, OR, NOT, IN
Basic string methods: Length, UpperCase, UpCase, Trim
Input validation (e.g. check for negative before sqrt)

cols.title: 📘 Theory (Paper 2)
items: Input devices: keyboard, mouse, scanner, microphone, biometric
Storage devices: HDD, SSD, USB, SD cards

blocks.title: Term 3 • July – September (PAT begins)
blocks.subtitle: Loops & full string methods; computer management & internet

cols.title: 🔬 Practical (Paper 1)
items: String handling from first principles (no built-in: count chars, find, remove, replace)
Full string methods: Copy, Delete, Insert, Pos, Left, Right

cols.title: 📘 Theory (Paper 2)
items: File management, housekeeping, disk cleanup, backup
WWW, URLs, web browsers, search engines
Email & internet threats: phishing, spam, hoaxes, POPIA

blocks.title: Term 4 • October – November (PAT due + exams)
blocks.subtitle: Internet services, PAT file I/O & revision

cols.title: 🔬 Practical (Paper 1)
items: PAT only: file I/O using LoadFromFile / SaveToFile
Revision of all T1–T3 practical work

cols.title: 📘 Theory (Paper 2)
items: Internet services: online storage, synchronisation, collaboration

runs.t: These topics are not explicitly required by CAPS but have appeared in NSC past papers (2015–2024). Knowing them can mean the difference between a pass and a distinction.
These Delphi functions, components and techniques are not listed in CAPS but appear regularly in exam questions or are needed to solve exam problems efficiently.

desc.t: Replaces all or first occurrence of a substring within a string.
s := StringReplace(s, 'old', 'new', [rfReplaceAll]);
A powerful list of strings — very useful for loading file lines into memory.
sl := TStringList.Create; sl.LoadFromFile('data.txt'); sl.Free;
Formats numbers/strings with precise control —
Format('%.2f', [rVal])
gives 2 decimal places. More powerful than FloatToStrF.
Generate random integers. Always call
Randomize;
first to seed the generator.
Random(100)
returns 0–99.
Let users browse for files at runtime.
if OpenDialog1.Execute then sFile := OpenDialog1.FileName;
Convert between TDate values and string representation. Used with TDateTimePicker.
sDate := DateToStr(dtpDate.Date);
Catch runtime errors gracefully — e.g. invalid StrToInt input.
try iNum := StrToInt(edtNum.Text); except ShowMessage('Invalid'); end;
Returns the number of lines in a Memo. Often used in loops to process all memo lines.
for i := 0 to Memo1.Lines.Count - 1 do
Case-insensitive string comparison — avoids problems when user types 'yes', 'YES' or 'Yes'.
if SameText(sInput, 'yes') then
Convert integers to hexadecimal strings and back. Sometimes tested in conjunction with number systems theory.
sHex := IntToHex(255, 4); // = '00FF'
Built-in boolean checks — cleaner than MOD 2.
if Odd(iNum) then // true if iNum is odd
Break
exits the loop immediately.
Continue
skips to the next iteration. Both appear in exam solutions — know the difference.

runs.t: These theory topics have appeared in NSC Theory papers but sit at the edge of or outside the formal CAPS scope. They typically appear in social implications or "current technology" questions.

desc.t: AI appears regularly in social implications questions — automation of jobs, AI decision-making, bias in AI, chatbots. Not deeply technical — understand the concept and its social effects.
A distributed digital ledger of linked blocks. Used in cryptocurrency, supply chain, and secure records. Appeared in Gr12 theory papers. Know: what it is, how it works, and 2 uses.
AI-generated fake videos or audio that appear authentic. Social implications question topic — impacts on trust, truth, and politics. Know the definition and two risks.
A part of the internet not indexed by search engines, accessed via special software (Tor). Used for anonymity — legal and illegal uses. Appeared in theory cybercrime questions.
The principle that all internet traffic should be treated equally — ISPs cannot throttle or prioritise certain content. A social/political IT topic.
The successor to IPv4 — uses 128-bit addresses (vs 32-bit) to solve address exhaustion. Format: 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334. Know why it was introduced.
RAID 0: speed (striping, no redundancy). RAID 1: mirroring (exact copy). RAID 5: striping with parity (balance of speed + fault tolerance). CAPS only mentions RAID in general — exams go deeper.

Hiding secret information within ordinary files (images, audio) without detection. Different from encryption — encryption scrambles data; steganography hides its existence. Appeared in theory questions.
Self-executing contracts where terms are written in code on a blockchain. No middlemen needed. Related to blockchain technology — appeared in Gr12 social implications.
Processing data close to where it is generated (the "edge" of the network) rather than sending it all to a central cloud server. Reduces latency — important for IoT and 5G.

All devices share a single communication line (the bus). Not in the current CAPS but appeared in older papers. Advantage: cheap. Disadvantage: single point of failure; performance drops with more devices.
Manipulating people (rather than systems) to reveal confidential information. Includes pretexting, baiting, tailgating. Appears in cybercrime theory questions.
Exams frequently ask you to
convert
values — not just explain systems. Practise: decimal↔binary, decimal↔hex, binary↔hex. Know the place values (powers of 2 and 16).
South African law governing how personal data must be collected, stored, and protected. Appears in privacy and legal implications questions. Know: what it is, who it protects, and 2 requirements.
SA legislation that criminalises hacking, ransomware, online fraud, and malicious communications. Often referenced in cybercrime theory questions for Gr12.
A virtual replica of a physical object or system, updated in real time from sensor data. Used in engineering, manufacturing, and smart cities. Appeared in 4IR/IoT questions.

runs.t: A comprehensive reference of all Delphi functions you need to know — both CAPS-required and commonly appearing in exams.

rows.t: Position of substring (0 if not found)
Insert text into string at position
base to the power of exp
String → Integer (safe — returns default if invalid)
Off-by-one errors in loops; forgetting to initialise variables
Using wrong index (0 vs 1-based); not closing file
Class with constructor, accessors, mutators, toString (~25–30 marks)
Calling Create on the object var (not class name); forgetting Result in functions
TADOQuery, SQL statements including JOIN (~20–25 marks)
Not calling qry.Close before changing SQL; wrong JOIN ON condition
Write a recursive function; trace it (~10–15 marks)
Missing base case; not returning Result
Correct normal form identification; proper primary/foreign key use
Balance of positive AND negative effects; specific examples
Correct crime type names; effects on both victim and business

runs.t: Read the question twice — "list" means bullet points, "explain" means a sentence or two
Never leave a practical question blank — partial marks are awarded for correct structure
In SQL: always check your WHERE condition matches the question's criteria exactly
In OOP: write the constructor first — it sets up all the attributes you'll use elsewhere
In theory: "advantage AND disadvantage" means you need both for full marks
